title: Spiky API with Pay-Per-Use Economics
description:
## Unpredictable Traffic, No Servers to Run

A startup is launching a public REST API for a mobile app. Traffic is **highly spiky and unpredictable** — it can sit near zero for hours, then jump to thousands of requests per second during a marketing push. The business wants to **pay only for what it uses**, run **no servers**, and have the backend scale automatically with demand. Each request does a small amount of compute and a read/write to a key-value store.

Which architecture BEST meets these requirements?

options.text: Amazon API Gateway in front of AWS Lambda functions, with Amazon DynamoDB for storage
An always-on fleet of EC2 instances behind an ALB, sized for the peak request rate
A single large EC2 instance running the API and a self-hosted database, with a CloudWatch alarm to scale vertically
Amazon ECS on EC2 running the API continuously with RDS for storage

explanation: For spiky, unpredictable, pay-per-use traffic with no servers to manage, the serverless pattern **API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB** is ideal: API Gateway handles the HTTP endpoint, throttling, and auth; Lambda scales out automatically per concurrent request and costs nothing when idle (true scale-to-zero); DynamoDB (on-demand capacity) scales the data layer the same way. To mitigate cold starts on latency-sensitive paths you can add **provisioned concurrency** (keeps a pool warm) or **reserved concurrency** (caps/guarantees concurrency for a function). Option B over-provisions for peak and pays for idle capacity. Option C is a single point of failure with manual scaling and no scale-to-zero. Option D keeps containers and an RDS instance running 24/7 — it does not match "no servers" or "pay only for what you use."

hints.title: Match the economics
hints.body: "Pay only for what you use" plus "near zero for hours" points to scale-to-zero compute, which rules out anything always-on.

hints.title: Cold starts
hints.body: The right answer has a known weakness (cold start latency). Knowing the mitigations — reserved vs provisioned concurrency — confirms it is still the correct choice.
